abstract: Large Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT) has shown strong performance on long-context 3D reconstruction, but its fully plastic inference-time updates remain vulnerable to catastrophic forgetting and overfitting. As a result, LaCT is typically instantiated with a single large chunk spanning the full input sequence, falling short of the broader goal of handling arbitrarily long sequences in a single pass. We propose Elastic Test-Time Training inspired by elastic weight consolidation, that stabilizes LaCT fast-weight updates with a Fisher-weighted elastic prior around a maintained anchor state. The anchor evolves as an exponential moving average of past fast weights to balance stability and plasticity. Based on this updated architecture, we introduce Fast Spatial Memory (FSM), an efficient and scalable model for 4D reconstruction that learns spatiotemporal representations from long observation sequences and renders novel view-time combinations. We pre-trained FSM on large-scale curated 3D/4D data to capture the dynamics and semantics of complex spatial environments. Extensive experiments show that FSM supports fast adaptation over long sequences and delivers high-quality 3D/4D reconstruction with smaller chunks and mitigating the camera-interpolation shortcut. Overall, we hope to advance LaCT beyond the bounded single-chunk setting toward robust multi-chunk adaptation, a necessary step for generalization to genuinely longer sequences, while substantially alleviating the activation-memory bottleneck.

citations.fullCitation: Tianyuan Zhang, Sai Bi, Yicong Hong, Kai Zhang, Fujun Luan, Songlin Yang, Kalyan Sunkavalli, William T Freeman, and Hao Tan. Test-time training done right. In International Conference on Learning Representations, 2026.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Large-Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT), the direct predecessor to the proposed LaCET method. The main paper's core contribution is an enhancement of the LaCT framework to address its stability issues, making this citation fundamental to understanding the work's context and motivation.
citations.title: Test-time training done right

citations.fullCitation: James Kirkpatrick, Razvan Pascanu, Neil Rabinowitz, Joel Veness, Guillaume Desjardins, Andrei A Rusu, Kieran Milan, John Quan, Tiago Ramalho, Agnieszka Grabska-Barwinska, et al. Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in neural networks. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 114(13): 3521–3526, 2017.
citations.justification: This work introduces Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC), which provides the core algorithmic inspiration for the 'Elastic' component in the paper's proposed Elastic Test-Time Training. The authors explicitly adapt the EWC regularizer to stabilize the fast-weight updates during inference.
citations.title: Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in neural networks

citations.fullCitation: Ziqiao Ma, Xuweiyi Chen, Shoubin Yu, Sai Bi, Kai Zhang, Chen Ziwen, Sihan Xu, Jianing Yang, Zexiang Xu, Kalyan Sunkavalli, et al. 4d-lrm: Large space-time reconstruction model from and to any view at any time. In Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is a key related work in the specific application domain of 4D reconstruction. The main paper's FSM-LRM architecture directly follows the parameterization and conditioning methods from 4D-LRM, making it a critical reference for the model's design choices.
citations.title: 4d-lrm: Large space-time reconstruction model from and to any view at any time

Learning to (learn at test time): Rnns with expressive hidden states. In International Conference on Machine Learning, pages 57503–57522, 2025.
citations.justification: This citation introduces the foundational concept of Test-Time Training (TTT), where a model's parameters are updated during inference. The main paper's method is an advancement within this TTT paradigm, making this a crucial reference for the underlying principles.
citations.title: Learning to (learn at test time): Rnns with expressive hidden states

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "Fast Spatial Memory with Elastic Test-Time Training."

---

### Research Report: Fast Spatial Memory with Elastic Test-Time Training

#### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by Ziqiao Ma, Xueyang Yu, Haoyu Zhen, Yuncong Yang, Joyce Chai, and Chuang Gan. The authors are affiliated with:
*   MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab
*   University of Michigan
*   University of Massachusetts Amherst

Ziqiao Ma and Xueyang Yu are noted as contributing equally to this work.

#### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The work addresses challenges within the field of 3D and 4D reconstruction, particularly focusing on learning spatiotemporal representations from long sequences of visual observations and rendering novel views at arbitrary times. This domain is crucial for applications such as 4D asset generation for entertainment industries (video games, film, AR/VR) and world modeling for embodied AI and robotics.

Recent advancements in Large Reconstruction Models (LRMs) and Large View Synthesis Models (LVSMs) have demonstrated progress in efficient and high-quality 3D/4D reconstruction, often leveraging Transformer-based sequence modeling to learn priors from large-scale multi-view data. However, a significant limitation of these models is their constraint by activation memory, which restricts their ability to process arbitrarily long sequences, especially in 4D scenarios where dynamic scenes evolve over extended periods. Existing models exhibit degraded reconstruction quality beyond their training context length, indicating limited temporal scalability.

Test-Time Training (TTT) and its variant, Large Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT), have been explored to address long-context issues in geometric reconstruction and view synthesis. LaCT enables chunk-wise, in-forward fast-weight adaptation, allowing a Transformer to recalibrate its internal representations during inference by updating small parameters based on key-value statistics without full backpropagation. While improving scalability, LaCT's fully plastic updates are prone to uncontrolled fast-weight drift, leading to catastrophic forgetting, overfitting, and instability during inference, particularly in dynamic 4D scenes where continuous changes occur. Consequently, LaCT is typically deployed with a single large chunk covering the entire input sequence, which limits its applicability to truly long sequences.

This research aims to advance beyond the limitations of existing LRM/LVSM models and LaCT by introducing a mechanism to stabilize fast-weight updates, enabling robust multi-chunk adaptation and generalization to genuinely longer sequences while mitigating memory bottlenecks. It positions itself as a development toward more scalable and efficient spatial memory architectures for 4D reconstruction.

#### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop an efficient, scalable, and robust model for 4D reconstruction that can learn spatiotemporal representations from long observation sequences and render novel view-time combinations. The work is motivated by several identified limitations in the current state of 3D/4D reconstruction and Test-Time Training:

1.  **Addressing Long-Context Limitations in 4D Reconstruction:** Existing LRMs and LVSMs are constrained by activation memory, making it challenging to process and learn from extended temporal sequences (e.g., long videos captured by moving cameras). Their performance degrades significantly when the input context exceeds training length, indicating a lack of temporal scalability. The goal is to overcome this limitation to handle arbitrarily long sequences in a single pass.
2.  **Mitigating Fast-Weight Drift and Overfitting in LaCT:** While Large Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT) improves TTT scalability by amortizing adaptation across chunks, its fully plastic fast-weight updates are unstable. In dynamic 4D scenes, this plasticity leads to uncontrolled parameter drift, resulting in catastrophic forgetting of previously learned information and overfitting to recent data during inference. This instability manifests as temporal ghosting artifacts or reduced generalization. The research aims to stabilize these updates.
3.  **Enabling Robust Multi-Chunk Adaptation:** Current LaCT implementations often rely on a single large chunk, which is not conducive to processing genuinely long or streaming sequences. A key motivation is to enable robust adaptation over multiple smaller chunks, which is essential for scaling to longer sequences and alleviating the activation-memory bottleneck.
4.  **Learning Scene-Level Spatiotemporal Representations:** The ambition is to design a spatial memory architecture that can compress visual observations across viewpoints and time into a unified 4D representation, thereby preserving both spatial structure and temporal dynamics for complex spatial environments.
5.  **Developing a Versatile and Efficient Model:** The objective includes creating a model, Fast Spatial Memory (FSM), that supports fast adaptation over long sequences, delivers high-quality 3D/4D reconstruction, can operate with smaller chunks, and mitigates issues like the camera-interpolation shortcut, while being compatible with various rendering decoders.

To achieve these objectives, the paper proposes Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET), inspired by principles from continual learning, to introduce a stability mechanism to LaCT's fast-weight updates.

#### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology centers on the development of Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET) and its integration into the Fast Spatial Memory (FSM) model architecture.

**4.1. Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET)**
LaCET enhances LaCT by incorporating an elastic regularization mechanism, inspired by Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) from continual learning. This mechanism stabilizes fast-weight updates to prevent catastrophic forgetting and overfitting.

*   **Core Principle:** LaCET adds a "consolidate" operation after the standard LaCT update. This operation introduces a Fisher-weighted quadratic penalty that pulls fast weights back towards a maintained "anchor state," preventing them from drifting excessively.
*   **Anchor Parameters (θ\*):** Each fast-weight module maintains a reference set of anchor parameters, which represent a consolidated past state.
*   **Importance Estimates (F):** The model continuously estimates the importance of each parameter using an online Fisher-style statistic. This importance matrix (F) is updated via an Exponential Moving Average (EMA) with a decay factor (α). Three variants for the statistic *S* were considered:
    *   **MAS-like:** Tracks the magnitude of the chunk-wise update.
    *   **EWC-like:** Emphasizes parameters with consistently large squared updates (φ(*S*c) = *S*c^2).
    *   **SI-like (Synaptic Intelligence):** Weights the update by its drift from the current anchor (φ(*S*c) = |*S*c|, *S*c = (θ'c - θc) ⊙ (θ'c - θ\*c)). The SI-like variant was empirically found to offer better training stability.
*   **Anchor Update Policies:** Different strategies for maintaining the anchor (θ\*) were investigated:
    *   **Global:** Anchors remain fixed to their initial state.
    *   **Streaming:** Anchors are reset to the current fast weights at each chunk boundary.
    *   **Streaming-EMA:** Anchors evolve as an exponential moving average of past fast weights (θ\* ← βθ\* + (1-β)θ). This policy acts as a low-pass filter on the fast-weight trajectory, balancing stability and plasticity, and was identified as the most effective for genuinely elastic memory behaviors.
*   **Consolidate Operator:** Formally, after an update producing intermediate fast weights θ'c, the elastic consolidation step is applied: θc+1 = θ'c - λ F_c ⊙ (θ'c - θ\*c), where λ controls the strength of the elastic prior.

**4.2. Fast Spatial Memory (FSM) Architecture**
FSM is an end-to-end feedforward network that integrates LaCET blocks to learn scene representations from visual observations.

*   **Input Tokenization:**
    *   Input consists of posed images {*I*j} from various view-time combinations, along with camera intrinsics and extrinsics.
    *   Camera parameters are converted into **Plücker ray maps** (*P*j), represented as [r_d, r_o × r_d], providing geometric information.
    *   Temporal conditioning is encoded via a **timestamp map** (*T*j), recording the normalized time of each frame.
    *   For each view, *I*j, *P*j, and *T*j are concatenated to form a per-view feature map (*e*I_j).
    *   These feature maps are partitioned into non-overlapping patches, flattened, and linearly projected into D-dimensional token embeddings.
*   **LaCET Backbone:** The core of FSM is a stack of LaCET blocks.
    *   The fast-weight network within each LaCET block is implemented as a **SwiGLU-MLP** without bias terms, consisting of three parameter matrices {θ1, θ2, θ3}.
    *   Only input-view tokens are processed through the Key-Value (KV) projections to generate gradients for the update operation, ensuring target-view tokens do not interact.
*   **Rendering Decoders:** FSM supports two decoding variants:
    *   **LVSM-style Rendering (FSM-LVSM):** Does not rely on an explicit scene representation. Target view-time queries are formed as empty image-token maps with metadata. These query tokens are processed jointly with input tokens, and a lightweight linear decoder reconstructs RGB patches directly from the output token embeddings.
    *   **LRM-style Rendering (FSM-LRM):** Adopts an explicit 4D scene representation, specifically 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS). It predicts pixel-aligned Gaussian Splatting primitives for a set of virtual view planes (using input views as virtual views) and then renders target views through rasterization. Each Gaussian primitive is parameterized by a 20-dimensional vector.
*   **Training Objectives:** The model is trained using photometric supervision, minimizing an image reconstruction loss that combines L2 (Mean Squared Error) loss and LPIPS (Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity) loss.

**4.3. Pretraining Strategy**
FSM was pre-trained on a curated large-scale dataset of 3D/4D data (Table 2), including RealEstate10K, DL3DV, PointOdyssey, Spring, DynamicReplica, Multi-Cam Video, and Stereo4D. A long-context curriculum was employed, gradually increasing input resolution, temporal span, and the dynamic number of input views during training.

#### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research provides both quantitative and qualitative findings, derived from ablation studies and benchmark evaluations.

**5.1. Ablation Studies (Section 4)**
Ablation studies were conducted using FSM-LVSM on the Stereo4D dataset with controlled settings (12-layer LaCET backbone, 32 input/target views, 128x128 resolution, 136-frame temporal window) to analyze LaCET's components.

*   **Generalization Improvement:** LaCET significantly reduces the generalization gap between training and test performance observed in vanilla LaCT, indicating improved information transfer across chunks and suppression of fast-weight drift. This suggests LaCT's tendency to overfit local pattern shortcuts is mitigated.
*   **Anchor Update Policies:**
    *   **Full-sequence setup (single chunk):** All anchor policies behave similarly, with consolidation acting as a minor second-order correction.
    *   **Global anchoring:** Provides stabilization but lacks temporal continuity.
    *   **Streaming anchoring (w/o EMA):** Regularizes within-chunk drift but lacks cross-chunk memory consolidation, leading to higher proneness to overfitting.
    *   **Streaming-EMA anchoring:** Demonstrated the most stable and genuinely elastic behavior. This policy, which updates anchors via an EMA of past fast weights, effectively penalizes cumulative drift relative to a dynamically evolving anchor, proving critical for stable performance (Table 1 shows Streaming-EMA with SI/EWC/MAS achieving PSNR around 29.8-29.9, compared to LaCT's 26.0-26.9).
*   **Importance Estimates:** SI-style and EWC-style importance estimates performed similarly well, slightly outperforming MAS-style in terms of PSNR, LPIPS, and SSIM (Table 1).
*   **Mitigation of Camera-Pose Interpolation Shortcuts:**
    *   **Sparse vs. Continuous Views (Figure 5):** When trained with 32 input images, LaCET consistently outperformed LaCT under sparse input views, demonstrating stronger generalization across time and space. LaCT's performance degraded sharply with increased sparsity, while LaCT (1 chunk) degraded more gracefully, but is not scalable.
    *   In the continuous-view regime (frame interpolation), LaCT exhibited a tendency to exploit short-range temporal redundancy rather than learning a true view-conditioned spatial representation, leading to performance collapse. LaCET showed a substantially smaller gap between discrete-view and continuous-view performance, indicating it is less prone to this interpolation-only solution and better preserves the ability to model long-range 4D dynamics.

**5.2. Novel View Synthesis Performance (Section 5.2)**
FSM was evaluated on 4D and 3D Novel View Synthesis (NVS) benchmarks.

*   **4D Novel View Synthesis (Table 3, Figures 6, 8, 9, 11):**
    *   On the Stereo4D dataset, FSM-LVSM achieved a PSNR of 32.16, LPIPS of 0.043, and SSIM of 0.931 (at 256x256 resolution), significantly outperforming prior rendering-based methods like MoVieS (PSNR 27.19, LPIPS 0.114, SSIM 0.888) and 4DGT (PSNR 24.62, LPIPS 0.102, SSIM 0.785). FSM-LRM also performed comparably to MoVieS.
    *   On the NVIDIA benchmark, FSM-LVSM achieved a PSNR of 23.90, LPIPS of 0.105, and SSIM of 0.747 (at 256x256 resolution), demonstrating the best performance among feed-forward approaches and approaching the performance of stronger optimization-based methods.
    *   These results suggest that LaCET effectively benefits dynamic scene modeling by maintaining consistent spatial information across time.
*   **3D Novel View Synthesis (Table 4, Figure 7, 12):**
    *   On the DL3DV-140 benchmark (256x256 resolution), FSM-LVSM achieved a PSNR of 26.69, LPIPS of 0.091, and SSIM of 0.846 for static scenes, performing comparably to state-of-the-art static 3D NVS models like tttLVSM (PSNR 26.90, LPIPS 0.185, SSIM 0.837).
    *   For dynamic scenes in DL3DV, FSM-LVSM achieved PSNR of 24.61, LPIPS of 0.118, and SSIM of 0.787.
    *   This demonstrates that LaCET blocks preserve strong capabilities even on static scenes where temporal memory is less critical.

**5.3. Additional Discussions (Appendix B)**
*   **Batch Inference:** The study noted that batch size affects adaptation dynamics rather than just throughput in TTT architectures, but empirically, the effect was minimal, leading to a fixed inference batch size of 1 in subsequent experiments.
*   **Decoder Comparison:** LVSM-style decoding (direct patch prediction) generally performed better than LRM-style decoding (explicit 4DGS modeling) with the same number of training steps. This suggests that while explicit representations may offer stronger generalization, they are harder to optimize and more computationally expensive.
*   **Temporal Encoding:** Explicit timestamp maps as time conditioning led to faster convergence and better performance compared to RoPE-style time conditioning, possibly due to a more direct optimization signal.
*   **Failure Cases (Figure 10):** Under large camera or view interpolation, the model may fail to update subject motion consistently, exhibiting stale gestures, partial motion patterns, or ghosting artifacts. This indicates persistent challenges in maintaining accurate space-time correspondence and motion consistency during extrapolation from challenging viewpoints.

#### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research contributes to the field of 3D/4D reconstruction and sequential modeling by introducing Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET), a novel approach designed to enhance the stability and scalability of fast-weight adaptation in Transformer-based models.

The primary significance lies in LaCET's ability to address critical limitations of previous Test-Time Training methods, particularly LaCT. By integrating an elastic regularization inspired by EWC, LaCET effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting and overfitting that typically occur during continuous, plastic updates over long sequences. This allows for robust multi-chunk adaptation, which is a necessary step for processing genuinely long sequences in a single forward pass and for applications requiring streaming data, thereby alleviating the activation-memory bottleneck inherent in many large models.

The proposed Fast Spatial Memory (FSM) model, powered by LaCET, demonstrates competitive and in some cases superior performance on 4D Novel View Synthesis benchmarks compared to existing methods. Its ability to generate high-quality novel views from long observation sequences and arbitrary timestamps signifies a step toward more capable 4D world modeling for embodied AI, robotics, and the generation of dynamic 4D assets for various media. The finding that LaCET reduces the model's tendency to exploit camera-pose interpolation shortcuts, promoting the learning of true view-conditioned spatial representations, is also notable for the development of more generalizable 4D reconstruction systems.

Potential impacts include:
*   **Enhanced 4D Content Creation:** Improved tools for generating dynamic 3D/4D assets for film, video games, and AR/VR applications, offering more efficient workflows and higher fidelity.
*   **Advanced Embodied AI and Robotics:** More robust world models that can accurately represent and predict dynamic environments from continuous streams of sensor data, leading to more intelligent agents.
*   **Scalable Sequence Modeling:** The principles of elastic test-time training could be extended to other domains requiring stable adaptation over long sequences, beyond 4D reconstruction.

However, the authors acknowledge several limitations, including current constraints on licensable training data and computational budget preventing full realization of the method's potential on arbitrarily long sequences. The model currently assumes posed input images, leaving joint pose estimation in dynamic scenes as a future challenge. Furthermore, while NVS performance is high, the work notes that it does not inherently guarantee geometric faithfulness or temporally consistent motion without additional explicit geometric supervision, indicating an area for future development to further enhance the utility of learned 4D representations.
overview:
## Addressing the Memory Bottleneck in 4D Reconstruction

Spatiotemporal reconstruction, often referred to as 4D reconstruction, involves modeling the geometry and appearance of dynamic scenes over time. This capability is essential for generating 4D assets in media, virtual reality, and developing world models for embodied AI and robotics. While Large Reconstruction Models (LRMs) have made progress in 3D reconstruction, extending these to 4D remains computationally expensive.

The primary challenge lies in processing long sequences of visual observations. Standard Transformer-based architectures are constrained by activation memory, which limits the length of the sequences they can process in a single pass. When a model's input context exceeds its training length, reconstruction quality typically degrades. Recent research introduced Large Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT) to address this by allowing a model to update small internal parameters—known as fast weights—during inference. However, these updates are often unstable. In dynamic scenes, fully plastic updates lead to "fast-weight drift," where the model overfits to recent frames and forgets previously learned spatial structures, resulting in temporal artifacts or a total collapse of the representation.

![Fast Spatial Memory Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.07350v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The architecture of Fast Spatial Memory (FSM) and the internal mechanism of the Large Chunk Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET) block. The model processes posed input images to predict novel views by stabilizing fast-weight updates via an elastic consolidation step.*

To resolve these instabilities, researchers have introduced Fast Spatial Memory (FSM), a model that utilizes a mechanism called Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET). By incorporating principles from continual learning, LaCET stabilizes the fast-weight adaptation process, enabling the model to handle long sequences across multiple chunks without catastrophic forgetting or parameter drift.

## The Problem of Fast-Weight Drift

In the context of Test-Time Training (TTT), a model is not static during inference. Instead, it processes input data in chunks and performs a gradient-based update on a subset of its weights. This allows the model to compress the information from the input sequence into its own parameters, effectively acting as a "spatial memory."

However, existing TTT methods like LaCT suffer from a lack of regularization. Because the weights are updated freely based on local statistics of the current chunk, they can drift significantly from their initial state. In 4D reconstruction, this manifests as a failure to maintain a consistent global coordinate system or the loss of structural details from earlier frames. This drift is especially problematic when dealing with dynamic objects or moving cameras, where the model might prioritize local temporal interpolation over a coherent 3D representation. Consequently, LaCT is typically forced to use a single large chunk, which reintroduces the memory bottleneck it was intended to solve.

## Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET)

The proposed solution, LaCET, introduces a "consolidate" operation after the standard weight update. This step is inspired by Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC), a technique used in continual learning to prevent a neural network from forgetting old tasks when learning new ones. In LaCET, this is applied to the fast-weight trajectory during a single inference pass.

The consolidation step introduces a quadratic penalty that pulls the updated weights back toward an "anchor state." This prevents the parameters from moving too far in directions that are not supported by the overall history of the sequence. The core operation for updating the weights from chunk $c$ to $c+1$ is defined as:

$$\theta_{c+1} = \theta'_c - \lambda F_c \odot (\theta'_c - \theta^*_c)$$

In this equation, $\theta'_c$ represents the weights after a standard gradient update, $\lambda$ is a hyperparameter controlling the strength of the consolidation, and $\odot$ denotes element-wise multiplication. Two critical components of this mechanism are the importance matrix $F_c$ and the anchor parameters $\theta^*_c$.

### Importance Estimates and Anchors

The importance matrix $F_c$ determines which weights are most critical to preserve. The model estimates this using an online statistic updated via an Exponential Moving Average (EMA). The researchers found that a variant inspired by Synaptic Intelligence (SI) was particularly effective. This method tracks the product of the weight update and the drift from the current anchor:

$$\phi(S_c) = |S_c| \text{ where } S_c = (\theta'_c - \theta_c) \odot (\theta'_c - \theta^*_c)$$

The anchor $\theta^*$ represents a stable reference of past knowledge. The research investigated several strategies for updating this anchor. The most effective policy, termed Streaming-EMA, allows the anchor to evolve slowly as a low-pass filter of the fast-weight trajectory:

$$\theta^* \leftarrow \beta \theta^* + (1-\beta) \theta$$

where $\beta$ is a decay factor. This policy balances plasticity—allowing the model to learn new scene information—with stability, ensuring it does not lose the global spatial context. This elastic behavior allows FSM to process sequences in multiple smaller chunks, significantly reducing memory overhead while maintaining high reconstruction quality.

## Fast Spatial Memory (FSM) Architecture

FSM is designed as an end-to-end feedforward network that integrates these LaCET blocks. The architecture is modular and can support different rendering decoders depending on the application requirements.

### Input Tokenization and Representation

The model takes a sequence of posed images as input. To ensure the model understands the underlying 3D geometry and temporal sequence, each input image is augmented with:
1.  **Plücker Ray Maps:** These maps represent each pixel as a ray in 3D space, providing explicit geometric conditioning for the model.
2.  **Timestamp Maps:** A normalized scalar value for each frame that encodes the relative time of the observation.

These components are concatenated and projected into a sequence of token embeddings. These tokens are then passed through a stack of LaCET blocks. Within each block, the fast-weight network is implemented as a SwiGLU-MLP. Crucially, the model only uses the input-view tokens to generate the gradients for the fast-weight updates, ensuring that target-view queries do not contaminate the internal memory.

### Decoding Strategies

FSM supports two primary decoding variants:
*   **FSM-LVSM:** Adopting a Large View Synthesis Model approach, this variant does not create an explicit geometric representation like a point cloud. Instead, it uses query tokens for the target view-time combinations and decodes RGB patches directly from the output embeddings using a linear layer.
*   **FSM-LRM:** This variant predicts an explicit 4D representation. It outputs 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) primitives. Each primitive is defined by its position, rotation, scale, opacity, and color, which are then rendered into the target view using traditional rasterization techniques.

## Experimental Findings

The performance of FSM was evaluated across several 3D and 4D benchmarks, including Stereo4D, NVIDIA, and DL3DV.

### 4D Scene Reconstruction

On the Stereo4D dataset, FSM-LVSM achieved a PSNR of 32.16, significantly higher than previous rendering-based 4D methods such as MoVieS (27.19) and 4DGT (24.62). Qualitative results showed that FSM successfully avoids "ghosting" artifacts—where dynamic objects appear as transparent or blurred streaks—which are common in models that fail to maintain temporal consistency.

![Comparison of 4D Reconstruction](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.07350v1/x8.png)
*Figure 8: Qualitative comparison on the Stereo4D dataset. FSM-LVSM preserves significantly more detail in dynamic regions compared to baselines like MoVieS and 4DGT.*

### Generalization and Stability

A key finding of the research was the mitigation of the "camera-pose interpolation shortcut." Standard LaCT models often learn to interpolate between nearby camera frames rather than building a true spatial representation. When tested on sparse views, LaCT performance often collapses. In contrast, LaCET demonstrated robust generalization. Even when input views were sparse, the elastic regularization ensured the model maintained a coherent representation of the scene.

Ablation studies confirmed that the Streaming-EMA anchor policy was essential for this stability. Without it, the model either suffered from drift (standard LaCT) or lacked the temporal continuity needed for dynamic scenes (global anchoring).

## Significance and Broader Impact

The introduction of FSM and LaCET represents a shift in how sequential models can adapt to long-context data. By stabilizing test-time training, the model can compress observations into a unified spatiotemporal representation without being limited by the quadratic memory scaling of standard attention mechanisms.

For robotics and embodied AI, this means agents can potentially build and maintain a "mental map" of their environment from a continuous stream of visual data. In content creation, it enables the efficient generation of 4D assets from long video sequences, such as handheld camera captures of dynamic performances.

Despite these achievements, the researchers noted certain limitations. The model currently requires posed input images, meaning the camera positions must be known beforehand. Additionally, while the model is efficient, its ability to maintain motion consistency during extreme temporal extrapolation—predicting motion far beyond the observed window—remains a challenging area for future work. Nonetheless, FSM provides a scalable foundation for learning complex 4D environments from extensive visual experience.

keyInsights: Elastic regularization, particularly with a Streaming-EMA anchor update policy, effectively stabilizes fast-weight dynamics in Test-Time Training, enabling robust multi-chunk adaptation and improved information transfer over long sequences.

Stabilized fast-weight updates help models learn more generalizable view-conditioned spatial representations, reducing the tendency to rely on camera-pose interpolation shortcuts and improving performance under sparse inputs.

The FSM architecture, with its LaCET backbone and combined Plücker ray/timestamp map inputs, can efficiently compress visual observations into unified 4D representations for high-quality novel view synthesis.
originalProblem: Existing Large Reconstruction Models (LRMs) and Large View Synthesis Models (LVSMs) struggle with processing arbitrarily long 4D sequences due to activation memory constraints, leading to performance degradation beyond training context length.

Previous Test-Time Training (TTT) methods like LaCT suffer from unstable fast-weight updates, causing catastrophic forgetting, overfitting, and instability, which limits them to single large chunks and prevents true long-sequence scalability.
Models often exploit camera-pose interpolation shortcuts, failing to learn generalized spatiotemporal representations, which hinders performance in scenarios with sparse or novel viewpoints.
results: FSM-LVSM achieved a PSNR of 32.16, LPIPS of 0.043, and SSIM of 0.931 on the Stereo4D dataset for 4D novel view synthesis, significantly outperforming prior rendering-based methods like MoVieS (PSNR 27.19).

Ablation studies demonstrated that LaCET significantly reduced the generalization gap and improved stability over vanilla LaCT, with the Streaming-EMA anchoring policy showing the most effective elastic memory behavior.

The model exhibited reduced susceptibility to camera-pose interpolation shortcuts, maintaining robust performance under sparse input views and achieving a substantially smaller performance gap between discrete-view and continuous-view regimes compared to LaCT.
solution: Introduces Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET), an enhancement to LaCT that incorporates an elastic regularization mechanism to stabilize fast-weight updates, mitigating catastrophic forgetting and overfitting during continuous adaptation.

LaCET employs a Fisher-weighted quadratic penalty that pulls fast weights towards a dynamically evolving "anchor state," using a Streaming-EMA policy to balance stability and plasticity across multiple chunks.

Integrates LaCET blocks into the Fast Spatial Memory (FSM) model, an end-to-end feedforward network that learns 4D scene representations from visual observations, utilizing Plücker ray maps and timestamp maps for spatiotemporal conditioning.
summary.summary: Researchers from MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab, University of Michigan, and University of Massachusetts Amherst developed Elastic Test-Time Training (LaCET) to stabilize fast-weight updates in Transformer-based models, enabling robust multi-chunk adaptation for long 4D sequences. Their Fast Spatial Memory (FSM) model, incorporating LaCET, achieved a PSNR of 32.16 and LPIPS of 0.043 for 4D novel view synthesis on the Stereo4D dataset.

title: Fast Spatial Memory with Elastic Test-Time Training
